coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of a new nanocomposite based on cobalt oxide and a new polymer dots derived from polyethylene glycol diacid as a high performance, ultra-stable symmetric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Alimola, Fariba
Arsalani, Nasser
Ahadzadeh, Iraj
coredata.dc:description: Due to industrial requirements for energy as well as environmental problems related to the use of fossil fuels, the supercapacitor has been the suitable alternative. Hence, in this study, the new nanocomposite was synthesized from polyethylene glycol diacid (COOH-PEG-COOH), cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co (OAc)2) by using the one-step method. In this method, the polyethylene glycol diacid forms polymer dots (PDs), is simultaneously composited with Co (OAc)2, and form the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. The new PDs-CoO nanocomposite was fully identified by various techniques. To accurately investigate the electrochemical performance, the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor and cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate symmetric supercapacitor were fabricated. The PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor has excellent electrical conductivity. Specific pseudocapacitance was calculated by the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and impedance (EIS) techniques. In all electrochemical methods, the specific pseudcapacitance of the nanocomposite is about five times higher than that of cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate due to the presence of the PDs. The PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor has a high specific pseudocapacitance, about 1020 F.g−1 at 1 A.g−1 current density, and very well cyclic stability of 98.7% capacitance retention over 10,000 cyclic voltammetry cycles at 1 A.g−1. Also, a low power system was designed to test the actual performance of the supercapacitor, which confirmed the results of the CV, charge/discharge (GCD), and EIS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Supercapacitor
Cobalt oxide
Charge/discharge
Impedance
Cyclic voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor
Low power system designed to confirm the actual performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) FT-IR spectra of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and PDs. b) The thermogravimetric curve of PDs-CoO nanocomposite.
The XRD pattern of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, XRD patterns of graphite, and graphene oxide.
a) UV–vis spectrum of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite b) The fluorescence spectra of the PDs and the PDs-CoO nanocomposite.
a) The FE-SEM image of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. b and c) The TEM image of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. d) The HRTEM image of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. e) The SAED image of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite.
a) EDS spectrum of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. b) Corresponding elemental mapping of O, Co, C.

a) Cyclic voltammogram of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, PDs, and Co (OAc)2 in the potential window 0 to 2.5 V with a scan rate of 50 MV.s−1. b) The cyclic voltammetry of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite device at different scan rates and in the window with a potential of 0–2.5 V. c) The cyclic voltammetry of the Co (OAc)2 device in the potential window of 0–2.5 V. d) The cyclic voltammetry of the PDs device in the potential window of 0–2.5 V. e) Cyclic stability of the PDs-CoO2 nanocomposite and Co (OAc)2.

a) The GCD analysis of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite various current densities from 1 to 5 A.g−1 in the potential window of 2.5 V. b) GCD analysis of the Co (OAc)2 in various current densities from 1 to 5 A.g−1 in the potential window of 1.2 V.

a) The Nyquist plots of two samples of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and Co (OAc)2. b) The high-frequency semicircle of two samples of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and Co (OAc)2. c) The equivalent electrical circuit inset.
Schematic of the PDs-CoO symmetric supercapacitor and LED illuminated by the PDs-CoO supercapacitor.
The schematic diagram of low power system used for supercapacitor investigation.
DLS and zeta potential of the samples.
Fitted parameters of EIS data for Co (OAC)2 and the PDs-CoO nanocomposite.
Low power system data of Co (OAc)2 and the PDs-CoO.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of a new nanocomposite based on cobalt oxide and a new polymer dots derived from polyethylene glycol diacid as a high performance, ultra-stable symmetric supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box, 51666, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aResearch Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box, 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Electrochemical Instrumentation and Energy Systems, Department of Physical Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box, 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Electrochemical Instrumentation and Energy Systems
Department of Physical Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bResearch Laboratory of Electrochemical Instrumentation and Energy Systems, Department of Physical Chemistry Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box, 51666, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to industrial requirements for energy as well as environmental problems related to the use of fossil fuels, the supercapacitor has been the suitable alternative. Hence, in this study, the new nanocomposite was synthesized from polyethylene glycol diacid (COOH-PEG-COOH), cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co (OAc)2) by using the one-step method. In this method, the polyethylene glycol diacid forms polymer dots (PDs), is simultaneously composited with Co (OAc)2, and form the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. The new PDs-CoO nanocomposite was fully identified by various techniques. To accurately investigate the electrochemical performance, the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor and cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate symmetric supercapacitor were fabricated. The PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor has excellent electrical conductivity. Specific pseudocapacitance was calculated by the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and impedance (EIS) techniques. In all electrochemical methods, the specific pseudcapacitance of the nanocomposite is about five times higher than that of cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate due to the presence of the PDs. The PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor has a high specific pseudocapacitance, about 1020 F.g−1 at 1 A.g−1 current density, and very well cyclic stability of 98.7% capacitance retention over 10,000 cyclic voltammetry cycles at 1 A.g−1. Also, a low power system was designed to test the actual performance of the supercapacitor, which confirmed the results of the CV, charge/discharge (GCD), and EIS.

ce:section.ce:para: The need for energy in modem societies and the environmental problems associated with fossil fuels consumption has led to increased demand for high-power energy conversion and storage systems using easy method [1]. Among energy storage devices, supercapacitors are powerful devices that are very valuable due to their high energy density [2], high charge and discharge speed, high power density, low weight, temperature performance limitation, environmental safety [3], and low cost [4]. The supercapacitor is the performance between the capacitor and the battery, which has a longer life cycle than conventional capacitors and has a higher power and energy density than batteries. Supercapacitors are divided into three categories based on charge storage: 1) electric double-layer capacitor (EDLC) is a common supercapacitor whose operation is due to the accumulation of electrostatic charge between ions located on the electrode/electrolyte surface, which have a long charge/discharge time, but due to their limited surface area, they have less energy than pseudocapacitance [5], 2) pseudocapacitance is based on electrically active phases at the interface between the electrode/electrolyte, 3) hybrid supercapacitor is a combination of EDLC and pseudocapacitance. Derivatives of elements such as Pd [6], Rh, and Ru have been used extensively as electrode materials in the construction of supercapacitors, but their use is not economically viable [7]. The cobalt element is one of the most popular candidates for making supercapacitors due to its relatively affordable and common [8]. To improve the electrochemical performance of cobalt, its derivatives such as cobalt oxides [9], cobalt hydroxide [10], MOF-Co, and cobalt polymetallic oxides, have been used in fabricating the supercapacitors [11]. Cao et al. investigated the performance of CoO-based supercapacitors and reported high electrical conductivity of CoO nanoparticles and a capacitance of about 600 F.g−1 at 1 F.g−1. Other studies have shown that the bandgap range of metal oxides, including CoO, has a small diameter that reduces the capacitance. To overcome this problem, carbon electrode materials such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene-like materials, and other carbon compounds were used to composite these compounds [12]. Quantum dots studied in recent years are a zero-dimensional material, and there are different functional groups on their surface [13]. The presence of these groups disperses the QDs in the solvent well and helps the electrolyte to penetrate, which ultimately increases the capacitance [14]. Sahoo et al. synthesized the NiS/CQD composite by hydrothermal method and found that the NiS/CQD composite has a higher specific capacity than the pristine NiS [15]. A. Soam et al. investigated the performance of the carbon-coated cobalt oxide (CCCO3O4) supercapacitor. They concluded that by coating carbon on cobalt oxide, the electrical conductivity increases due to the electron transfer path, and the electrochemical performance of the electrode was improved [16]. Quantum dots divide into three categories based on carbon: carbon dots, graphene quantum dots, and polymer dots [17]. Polymer dots (PDs) were a new type of carbon nanomaterial or a zero-dimensional polymer-based nanomaterial. Polymer dots are synthesized with two separate classes of conjugated [18] and non-conjugated polymers [19]. The PDs attracted the attention of many researchers due to their owing polymer chains and functional groups at the surface. Also, polymer dots have unique properties, including easy modification and biocompatibility. So, they are widely used in solar cells, gas sensors, biosensors, and especially in supercapacitors [20, 21]. In order to improve the performance of PDs in many applications such as optoelectronics, multi-imaging, and nanomedicine, it is necessary to be combined with two or several different nanoparticles [22]. In fact, by combining PDs with nanomaterials, the particle size can be controlled, which improves its properties. For example, the poly [9,9´ -dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-co 4,7-benzo-thiadiazole] (PFBT) combined with CdSe quantum dots, and the optical properties of the polymer dots were improved significantly [23]. In another example, the PDs were composited with lead sulfide quantum dot to improve photovoltaic applications [23]. Despite the excellent electrical conductivity and specific capacity of PDs compounds, to date, there is not so vast study on the performance of these compounds in supercapacitors. In this study, for the first time, new PDs-CoO nanocomposite was synthesized using green substances, polyethylene glycol diacid (COOH-PEG-COOH 600) and Co (OAc)2, in one step. The synthesis method was one step with the hydrothermal method. The PDs-CoO nanocomposite was fully identified using related analyzes. The performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitors first was investigated. The analysis results i.e., charge/discharge (GCD), impedance (EIS), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were compared with that of Co (OAc)2, which showed that the specific capacity of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was about five times higher than that of Co (OAc)2. To evaluate the actual performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor, this supercapacitor was employed in a low power evaluating system which confirmed the result of CV, GCD, and EIS.

Polyethylene glycol diacid (600) and cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co (CH3COO)2 0.4 H2O) were provided from Sigma-Aldrich. Potassium hydroxide (KOH), dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol and, carbon black were purchased from Merck and used without purification.

The 1 mmole (600 mg) of polyethylene glycol diacid 600 was dissolved in 12 mL of ethanol and 12 mL of dimethylformamide and placed at an ultrasonic bath for 15 min to be fully solved. Then, the mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and placed in the oven at 140 °C for 30 h. Then, the obtained polymer dots were dialyzed against distilled water for 24 h and freeze-drying.

In order to synthesis of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, polyethylene glycol diacid and cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate (Co (CH3COO)2 0.4 H2O) in a ratio of 1:4 M were dissolved in ethanol/DMF (1/1) and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min, and the resulting solution was poured in Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave and placed in the furnace at 140 °C for 30 h. The purple residue was washed three times with dimethylformamide followed by chloroform and placed in an oven at 60 °C for three hours to dry completely.

The quantum yield of PDs was calculated by formula 1.
(1)
Φ
c
=
Φ
r
(
A c
/
A r
)
(
I c
/
I r
)
(
η c
/
η r
)
where the ϕ is quantum yield, A is the area below the emission curve, I is the excitation wavelength, η is the solvent refractive index, and subtitles c and r: refer to PDs and resources (quinine sulfate). Quinine sulfate with a quantum yield of 54 in 0.1 M sulfuric acid was used as a reference [24].

The Infrared spectroscopy (IR) was measured using a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed in the temperature range 0–700 °C under argon gas with a thermal analysis system. UV–vis spectroscopy was used to measure the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the solutions. A fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the new synthesized PDs. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurement of nanocomposites was carried out using a DLS instrument. The X-ray diffraction pattern was record using an X-ray instrument. To processed the X-ray diffraction pattern, the Philips X'Pert Pro-software was used. Nanocomposite morphology was performed by two analyze, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). For CV, GCD and EIS measurements, an Autolab electrochemical analyze was used.

For electrochemical analysis, the symmetric supercapacitor was designed as a two-electrode system. In the construction of symmetrical supercapacitors, the nanocomposite and Co (OAc)2 as active material, a pair of copper plates as current collector, glass microfiber paper as separators, and 2 M KOH as electrolytes were used. Specific capacity was calculated using the following formulae:
(2)
C =
∫
iVdV
μ m
Δ
V
where,
∫
iVdV
is generally the area below the CV curve diagram where V and i are potentials and currents in CV analysis.
Δ
V
is the potential window, m is the mass of active substance used, and μ is the potential scan rate [25, 26].

Fig. 1 a shows the FTIR spectra of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and PDs. The band observed at 3444 cm −1 is related to the stretching vibration of OH (OCOH and OH) and the band at 1100 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of COH. The wide band observed at 1218–1418 cm−1 can be related to the bending vibration of COH. This confirms the presence of -OH groups on the surface of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. The bands observed at 1620 cm−1and 2866 cm−1 can be related to CO and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. The weak band observed at 785 cm−1 was could be attributed to the bending vibrations of pHH. The band observed at 671 cm−1 was attributed to Co-O vibration (The FTIR spectrum of cobalt oxide was referenced in Fig. 1S for comparison) [27].

Fig. 1b shows the thermogravimetric spectra of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. Thermogravimetric of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was performed under argon gas from 30 to 700 °C. As can be seen, in the temperature range of 30 to 180 °C, 4% of the weight was decreased due to the removal of water. After 120 °C, the weight was suddenly decreased by 43% due to the removal of the PDs and the surface functional groups on the PDs such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic acid, and the removal of other phenyl groups [28]. Also, a small amount of weight loss was related to the residual PEG that was the precursor to the formation of the nanocomposite [28].

Fig. 2 shows the XRD spectrum of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and XRD spectra of graphite and graphene oxide for comparison. In the XRD spectrum of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, one peak at 2θ of 12.6 corresponding to the (001) plane was observed, which was similar for the graphene oxide diffraction peak (at 2θ of 10.3). This increase may be due to the presence of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylat the edge of the crystal plates, which increased the distance of the sp3 layer in the nanocomposite structure. The peak observed at 25.59 (corresponding to the (002) plane) is the same as the graphite peak at 26.6, which shifts and widens due to the increased distance of the sp2 layer [29]. The two peaks observed at 25.5 and 12.6 confirmed the synthesis of the PDs. The peak observed in 2θ = 36.54, 42.44, 61.53, 73.82, and 77.66 are corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) of cobalt oxide [30], so the presence of this peak confirms the presence of cobalt oxide in the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. Using the Debye-Scherrer,s equation, the size of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was found to be about 16 nm.

Fig. 3 shows the optical properties of the PDs and the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. According to the UV–vis spectrum of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite (Fig. 3a), the PDs-CoO nanocomposite showed an adsorption peak at 226 nm (λAb) and a wide adsorption peak at 588–598 nm (λAb). The UV–vis spectrum of the CoO was referenced in Fig. 2S [31].The behavior of PL and the fluorescence properties of the PDs and the PDs-CoO nanocomposite were compared in Fig. 3b. The PDs and the PDs-CoO nanocomposite were excited at 380 nm (λem) wavelengths, that the PDs and the PD-CoO nanocomposite emitted at 418 nm (λem) and 425 nm (λem). The level of the PL curve is asymmetric for PDs, which is consistent with similar reports. that by the combination of the PDs and Co (OAc)2 and the formation of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, the PL curve was completely become symmetric, which shows that the ingredients were excellently combined [32].

After reviewing the results of the PL of the PDs-CoO, the quantum yield of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was calculated at about 59%. The high quantum yield is somehow effective in improving electrochemical properties and increasing specific capacitance [33]. The microstructure, morphology, and elemental distribution of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, were performed with FE-SEM, HRTEM, EDS, and elemental map analyses. Fig. 4 a shows the FE-SEM image of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite that the morphology of the particles is hollow woven spheres. The SEM of CoO for comparison was referenced in Fig. 3S [30]. Fig. 4b shows the TEM image of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. The morphology of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite in the TEM image is consistent with the morphology shown in the FE-SEM image, which is hollow woven spheres. According to the TEM image (Fig. 4c), the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was synthesized with a particle size of about 30–50 nm which is consistent with the results obtained from DLS. Fig. 4d shows the HRTEM image of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. The HRTEM image depicted a lattice margin of 0.28 and 0.1 corresponding to plates (111) and (001), which confirms the presence of CoO nanoparticles and the PDs in the PDs-CoO nanocomposite. The woven structure of the nanocomposite (shown in Fig. 4a and b) increases the electrochemical activity due to the faster transfer of electrons and ions. Fig. 4e shows the SAED image of the nanocomposite. As can be seen, the PDs-CoO nanocomposite has a polycrystalline structure [34, 35].

Fig. 5 a and b shows the EDX analysis and the map analysis for the elements O, Co, and, C. The distribution of O, Co, C elements in the structure of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was shown in Fig. 5a by which the homogeneously successful synthesis of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite confirms. According to the distribution of elements related to EDX analysis in Fig. 5a, the result of TGA analysis was confirmed.

Table 1 shows the DLS data and zeta potential of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and the PDs. The PDs-CoO nanocomposite particle size was approximately 42 nm. Also, the PDs particle size was 1.6 nm. Additionally, the zeta potentials of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and the PDs were −2 and −58, respectively. As stated, the zeta potential of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was more positive than the PDs, because the PDs are composited with Co (OAc)2 and formed the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, which subsequently reduced the negative charge [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the electrochemical properties of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite were investigated using CV, GCD, and EIS techniques. All of the electrochemical studies are based on symmetric supercapacitors. A symmetric supercapacitor was prepared from the PDs-CoO nanocomposite material to investigate the electrochemical performance. Also, to investigate the effect of the PDs on the electrochemical performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, a symmetric supercapacitor was prepared using Co (OAc)2. The KOH (2 M) and the copper plates were used as the electrolyte and the current collector and specified amount of active materials, respectively, in both symmetric supercapacitors.

Fig. 6 a shows the cyclic voltammogram of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor and Co (OAc)2 symmetric supercapacitor in the potential window from 0 to 2.5 V with a scan rate of 50 MV.s−1. By comparing these voltammograms, it was found that the current density of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite is five times higher than that the Co (OAc)2. This increase is due to the presence of PDs, which increases the contact areas of the electrode/electrolyte with the electron, or in other words, reduces the transmission path and ion diffusion length. The increased current density of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite compared to Co (OAc)2 can also be attributed to the synergistic effect between Co (OAc)2 and the PDs. Other reasons of the increase of the current density for the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor may be the higher conductivity and higher electroactive levels [37]. An accurate CV comparison of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device with the Co (OAc)2 symmetric device showed that there were two cathode peaks and two anode peaks in the CV of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device, which was attributed to the Co2+/Co3+ pair [38]. The presence of anodic and cathodic peaks confirms the faradic current in the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device. According to the CV analysis of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device, it exhibits the psudocapacitor property [39]. Fig. 6b shows the cyclic voltammetry of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device at different scan rates and in the potential window of 0–2.5 V. As can be seen, the current density has increased with the increasing scan rate, and this is due to the increased capacitive current [40]. Fig. 6c shows the cyclic voltammetry of the Co (OAc)2 symmetric device in the potential window of 0–2.5 V. The current density increased with increasing scan rate but with a much less rate of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device. This change in current density has somehow confirmed the formation of the PDs and their composite with Co (OAc)2. The special capacitance of Co (OAc)2 symmetric supercapacitor, the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor was calculated 220 F.g−1 and 1020 F.g−1 by cyclic voltammetry, respectively. To confirm the synergistic effect between Co (OAc)2 and PDs, the symmetric device of PDs was prepared and its cyclic voltammetry at different scan rates was shown in Fig. 6d. An important factor in electrochemical evaluation is the investigation of cycling stability. By measuring cyclic voltammetry in the range of 0–2.5 V at 1 A.g−1current density, the cyclic stability to 10,000 cycles were investigated. Fig. 6e shows the cyclic stability. As can be seen, the capacitive capacity of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device was decreased only 3.4% after 10,000 cycles, which confirms the cyclic stability of 96.7%.

Fig. 7 a, b shows GCD analysis for the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor, and Co (OAc)2 symmetric supercapacitor at various current densities from 1 to 5 A.g−1 in potential windows of 2.5 V and 1.2 V. By comparing the two GCD curves of 7a and 7b, it is clear that the discharge time of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device was longer than the Co (OAc)2 symmetric device. Also, the potential window of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device is wider than the Co (OAc)2 symmetric device. In both symmetric devices, the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and Co (OAc)2 specific capacity decreases with current density increasing. [41, 42]. The GCD of the cobalt acetate symmetric device shows the faradic current, but the GCD of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device showed a pseudocapacitive current. The pseudocapacitive current of PDs-CoO nanocomposites is due to the polymeric and carbon properties of PDs in the PDs-CoO nanocomposite structure [43].

Complete electrochemical information on the electrochemical behavior of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was obtained by impedance measurement. Fig. 8 a shows the Nyquist plots of two samples of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric device and Co (OAc)2 symmetric device. As observed, there is a semicircle at high frequency and a rather vertical line at low frequency. As long as the straight-line shifts at a low frequency towards the vertical wall, it confirms the ideal capacitance behavior. The high-frequency semicircle, shown as the insert in Fig. 8b, represents the faradic reactions and charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte surface [44, 45]. To fit and analyze impedance data, in other words, to find a suitable equivalent circuit to fit the impedance data and the values of each of the parameters of that equivalent circuit according to the Nyquist spectrum, as well as the physical chemistry image of the system, an equivalent circuit was suggested according to Fig. 8c. This circuit consists of four electrical elements Rs, CPE1-T, Rct, CPE2-T represent the equivalent series resistance, including copper collector resistance, membrane resistance, and KOH electrolyte resistance injected in the supercapacitor, as well as resistance electric of each electrode. Rs shows itself as a point on the z-axis or the actual resistance in the Nyquist diagram at high frequencies. The element CPE1 is a constant phase element representing the non-ideal electrical double layer where the electrodes and the KOH solution come into contact. Rct the charge transfer resistance is related to the faradic reactions on the surface of the electrodes, the possible oxidation or reduction of electroactive species on the surface of each of the two electrodes a supercapacitor. CPE2-T represents constant phase elements related to the non-ideal behavior of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite that adsorbs KOH solution. The results of fitting the impedance data on the equivalent circuit using the demonstration version of ZView software are reported in Table 2 in which, as can be seen, the unit of Rs was (Ω), the unit of Rct was (Ω.cm2), and CPE1-P, CPE2-P are dimensionless numbers that indicate the deviation of the capacitive behavior from the ideal state. It should be noted that the value of the P parameter closer to 1, the more ideal the capacitor [46, 47]. CPE1-T and CPE2-T have units of S. sp where S represents siemens or Ω−1. According to Table 2, it can be observed that the amount of CPE1-T and CPE2-T elements of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite was higher than that of Co (OAc)2, which showed, with the formation of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, the capacitive behavior improved and the specific capacitance increased. The specific capacitance has increased from about 118 F.g−1 for Co (OAc)2 to 300 F.g−1 for the PDs-CoO nanocomposite, so the impedance behavior confirmed the CV results.

Scheme 1 shows the boost converter connected to the PDs-CoO tow-electrode supercapacitor, which eventually turned on a white LED. In Scheme 2 schematic diagram of the symmetric the PDs-CoO supercapacitor was shown. The PDs-CoO nanocomposite as the cathode and anode of the device was used, which was separated by glass microfiber paper. A pair of copper foils as the current conductor and 2 M KOH solution as the electrolyte were used [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low power system designed to confirm the actual performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, prototype capacitors based on the PDs-CoO nanocomposite and Co (OAc)2 were investigated to evaluate the efficiency and performance of electrical energy storage. For this purpose, they were used to power a continuous wave (CW) transmitter module in the frequency range of 98–100 MHz. This module works with 1.5 V DC input voltage, and a current of about 50 μA. A boost voltage converter and an IC-based voltage regulator system were used to couple the two electrode supercapacitors to this module the input voltage from the supercapacitor in the range of 0.3–1.2 V is converted to a stable voltage of 1.5 V to start the transmitter module CW. The operation of the transmitter module was monitored by an FM receiver located 50 cm from it. A summary of the performance of these two prototypes of supercapacitors in this test was given in Table 3 . The schematic diagram of the used setup is depicted in Scheme 2.

Therefore, it can be concluded that the supercapacitor, especially supercapacitor based on the PDs-CoO nanocomposite with a capacity of about 5 times higher than Co (OAc)2, can be used as a proper power supply for low power systems.

The good electrical conductivity of carbon materials as well as improving the performance of carbon-based supercapacitors were encouraged us to investigate a new class of carbon-based materials called a polymer dot as a supercapacitor. Briefly, the new PDs-CoO nanocomposite was synthesized via a one-step and straightforward method using polyethylene glycol diacid and Co (OAc)2. In this method, polyethylene glycol diacid is converted into a polymer dot and simultaneously composited with Co (OAc)2. For electrochemical investigations, the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor and the Co (OAc)2 symmetric supercapacitor were fabricated. Examination of CV, GCD, and EIS techniques showed that the specific capacitance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor is about five times more that of Co (OAc)2 supercapacitor. The specific capacity calculated by the CV technique showed that the specific capacity was increased from about 200 F.g−1 for Co (OAc)2 to 1020 F.g−1 for the PDs-CO nanocomposite at 1 A.g−1 current density. Also, cyclic capability, charge transfer properties, and cyclic stability in the PDs-CoO nanocomposite symmetric supercapacitor increased compared to Co (OAc)2 symmetric supercapacitor. From this comparison, it can be concluded that the increase in specific capacity was due to the synthesis of the polymer dots from polyethylene glycol and its simultaneous combination with Co (OAc)2 by the hydrothermal synthesis method. The actual performance of the PDs-CoO nanocomposite supercapacitor of energy storage in a low power system was investigated which confirmed the results of CV, GCD, and EIS techniques. It can be concluded from the studies that the new PDs-CoO nanocomposite is an excellent material for low power system. From the above results, it can be concluded that the PDs-CoO nanocomposite due to the easy one-step synthesis method and use of nontoxic materials such as polyethylene glycol as well as excellent electrochemical properties such as high capacitance and excellent cyclic stability could be a promising material for electrochemical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz for the financial support for this research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140283.
